content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 4, 1992

James Zuchelli, Chair
Californians for
Constitutional Government
P.O. Box 1107
Pleasanton, CA 94566

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-19

Dear Mr. Zuchelli:

You have requested advice on behalf of Californians for Constitutional Government ("CCG") under the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

Are the following activities of CCG regulated by the Act?

1. Publication of a newsletter that endorses some candidates and opposes others.

2. Receipt of funds ($25) from an officeholder who is later endorsed by CCG.

3. A member of CCG's organizational group runs for elective office and is endorsed by CCG.

CONCLUSIONS

Californians for Constitutional Government is not required to register or file disclosure reports under the Act at this time. However, if CCG receives or spends $1,000 or more in a calendar year for the purpose of influencing the voters to vote for or against specific candidates or ballot measures, it will qualify as

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

James Zuchelli
A-92-19
Page 2

a "committee" under the Act and will incur campaign disclosure
obligations.

If CCG qualifies as a committee, funds received from
candidates, officeholders, or other persons to help pay the costs
of publishing the newsletter must be disclosed as "contributions
received" on the committee's campaign statements. Expenditures
made in connection with endorsing specific candidates in the
newsletter must be reported as "contributions" to the candidates
if such expenditures are made "at the behest" of the candidates.
Expenditures for communications which expressly advocate election or defeat of specific candidates but which are not made "at
the behest" of such candidates are reported as "independent
expenditures." In addition, CCG may qualify as a "controlled"
committee if one of its directors or officers becomes a candidate
for state or local elective office.

FACTS

Californians for Constitutional Government is an organization
that does not make monetary contributions to candidates. The
organization supports and opposes various candidates via a
newsletter. In a telephone conversation on January 23, 1992, you
clarified that at this time the organization has not received or
spent $1,000. However, future fundraising efforts may result in
the organization qualifying as a committee.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires that all "committees" file periodic reports
disclosing contributions received and expenditures made. (Sections 84101, 84200-84206.) "Committee" means any person or
combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year; or

(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year; or

(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or committees.

Sections 82015 and 82025 define "contribution" and
"expenditure" in part as payments made for political purposes.
Regulations 18225 (copies enclosed) provide that a payment is made for "political purposes" if it is for the purpose of
influencing the action of the voters for or against the nomination
or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or

James Zuchelli
A-92-19
Page 3

passage of any measure, or is received by, made at the behest of,
or made by:

     (A)  A candidate, unless it is clear from the surrounding
          circumstances that the payment was made or received for
          personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or
          status as an officeholder;

     (B)  A controlled committee; or

     (C)  An organization formed or existing primarily for
          political purposes. (Regulations 18215(a) and
          18225(a).)

     "Made at the behest" of a candidate means that the payment
was made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with, or at the request or
suggestion of the candidate. (Regulation 18215(b).)

     Therefore, if CCG's newsletter endorses candidates for elec-
tive office at the behest of those candidates, the costs of
publishing the newsletter are "contributions" to the candidates
and donations received by CCG to publish the newsletter are
"contributions" to CCG.

     If the newsletter supports or opposes candidates but does not
do so at the behest of any officeholder, candidate or committee,
the costs of publishing the newsletter may be "independent
expenditures" and donations received by CCG to publish the
newsletter will be "contributions" to CCG.2 An "independent
expenditure" is a payment in connection with an express advocacy
communication which expressly advocates the election or defeat of a
clearly identifiable candidate or measure, but which is not made at
the behest of the affected candidate or committee. (Section 82031.)

     A communication "expressly advocates" the nomination,
election or defeat of a candidate or the qualification, passage or
defeat of a measure if it contains express words of advocacy such as
"vote for," "elect," "support," "cast your ballot,"
"vote against," "defeat," "reject," "sign petitions for" or
otherwise refers to a clearly identified candidate or measure so
that the communication, taken as a whole, unambiguously urges a
particular result in an election.

                                        Regulation 18225(b)(2).


2    However, please see the exception for regularly published
newsletters contained in Regulation 18225(b)(4)(c).

James Zuchelli
A-92-19
Page 4

Therefore, if CCG receives "contributions" or makes
"expenditures" totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year, it will
qualify as a committee under Section 82013(a) and must file
recipient committee campaign statements. In addition, if a director or officer of CCG subsequently becomes a candidate, CCG may
become a "controlled" recipient committee.

"Controlled committee" means a committee which is controlled
directly or indirectly by a candidate or state measure proponent
of which acts jointly with a candidate, controlled committee or
state measure proponent in connection with making of expenditures.
A candidate or state measure proponent controls a committee if he,
his or her agent or any other committee he controls has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee. (Section
82016.) The Commission has interpreted the definition of
"controlled committee" very broadly to include any significant
participation by a candidate, his or her agent, or representatives
of any other committee he or she controls in the actions of a committee. (Davis Advice Letter, No. I-90-173.3)

The Act restricts the number and the types of committees that
can be controlled by a candidate. In some cases, candidate-
controlled committees are prohibited from making contributions or
expenditures to support or oppose other candidates. If CCG
qualifies as a committee, please contact this office again for
information concerning its specific filing requirements, the types
of forms required to be filed, as well as the restrictions imposed
on candidate-controlled committees.

If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact
me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Sandra L. Taylor
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

3 Copies of Commission advice letters may be obtained from the
Commission at a cost of 10¢ per page.

a "committee" under the Act and will incur campaign disclosure obligations.

sections.analysis:
The Act requires that all "committees" file periodic reports disclosing contributions received and expenditures made. (Sections 84101, 84200-84226.) "Committee" means any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees.

Sections 82015 and 82025 define "contribution" and "expenditure" in part as payments made for political purposes. Regulations 18215 and 18225 (copies enclosed) provide that a payment is made for "political purposes" if it is for the purpose of influencing the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or

passage of any measure, or is received by, made at the behest of, or made by:

(A) A candidate, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was made or received for personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as an officeholder;

(B) A controlled committee; or

(C) An organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes. (Regulations 18215(a) and 18225(a).)

"Made at the behest" of a candidate means that the payment was made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of the candidate. (Regulation 18215(b).)

Therefore, if CCG's newsletter endorses candidates for elective office at the behest of those candidates, the costs of publishing the newsletter are "contributions" to the candidates and donations received by CCG to publish the newsletter are "contributions" to CCG.

If the newsletter supports or opposes candidates but does not do so at the behest of any officeholder, candidate or committee, the costs of publishing the newsletter may be "independent expenditures" and donations received by CCG to publish the newsletter will be "contributions" to CCG.2 An "independent expenditure" is a payment in connection with a communication which expressly advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the passage or defeat of a clearly identified ballot measure, but which is not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate or committee. (Section 82031.)

A communication "expressly advocates" the nomination, election or defeat of a candidate or the qualification, passage or defeat of a measure if it contains express words of advocacy such as "vote for," "elect," "support," "cast your ballot," "vote against," "defeat," "reject," "sign petitions for" or otherwise refers to a clearly identified candidate or measure so that the communication, taken as a whole, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election.

Regulation 18225(b)(2).

2 However, please see the exception for regularly published newsletters contained in Regulation 18225(b)(4)(c).

Therefore, if CCG receives "contributions" or makes "expenditures" totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year, it will qualify as a committee under Section 82013(a) and must file recipient committee campaign statements. In addition, if a director or officer of CCG subsequently becomes a candidate, CCG may become a "controlled" recipient committee.

"Controlled committee" means a committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate or state measure proponent or which acts jointly with a candidate, controlled committee or state measure proponent in connection with making of expenditures. A candidate or state measure proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or any other committee he controls has a significant influence on the actions or decisions of the committee. (Section 82016.) The Commission has interpreted the definition of "controlled committee" very broadly to include any significant participation by a candidate, his or her agent, or representatives of any other committee he or she controls in the actions of a committee. (Davis Advice Letter, No. I-90-173.3)

The Act restricts the number and the types of committees that can be controlled by a candidate. In some cases, candidate-controlled committees are prohibited from making contributions or expenditures to support or oppose other candidates. If CCG qualifies as a committee, please contact this office again for information concerning its specific filing requirements, the types of forms required to be filed, as well as the restrictions imposed on candidate-controlled committees.
